metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In D1 and AC3(c), state the exact abort contract for bind_pending persist failure: name the exception type raised (or confirm it's caught internally), specify whether it increments mutation_failures or is routed to alert_store (or both), and state the minimum log level — e.g. 'raises BindPersistError (counted as mutation_failure, NOT alert_store); logged at ERROR with structured field persist_failure=True'.
findings.finding: AC3 sub-case (c) states 'bind_pending persist failure aborts + alerts' but neither the D1 design section nor the Scope section specifies the precise abort mechanism: does the failure raise a named exception type (e.g. PersistenceError), is it recorded as a mutation_failure (incrementing the exit-gate counter), does it call alert_store (as recovery failures do), or does it only emit a structured log? An implementing agent must guess, and test_write_ahead_recovery.py must assert something specific.
findings.location: Design decision D1 ('bind_pending persist failure is FATAL for that create (abort + loud error)'); AC3 sub-case (c); Scope section for dispatch_one.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add to D1: 'A bind_pending persist failure raises BindPersistError (or the existing store exception); it is counted as a mutation_failure (same exit-gate counter as other mutations) and logged at ERROR with persist_failure=True. It does NOT call alert_store — alert_store is reserved for recovery-path failures that are fail-open.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC5 to name four distinct test cases: (1) steal returns new oid → proceed; (2) steal returns None + still-held → exit 3; (3a) steal returns None + freed + acquire wins → proceed; (3b) steal returns None + freed + acquire lost to racer → exit 3 — and update 'All three cases' to 'All four cases' in the proof line.
findings.finding: AC5 enumerates three steal() outcome cases but omits the two sub-outcomes of case 3 (steal returns None AND ref is freed): the plan specifies 'attempt a normal acquire_pass_lock' but does not state whether BOTH sub-outcomes of that attempt — (a) acquire succeeds, proceed; (b) acquire lost to a racer, exit 3 — are tested, leaving one branch potentially uncovered.
findings.location: AC5 (Held-lock path); Design decision D3 ('if now free ... attempt a normal acquire_pass_lock (proceed if won, exit 3 if lost to a racer)')
findings.suggested_fix: In AC5 replace 'None + freed (ref released during our sleep) -> normal acquire_pass_lock attempt' with 'None + freed -> acquire_pass_lock attempt: (3a) won -> proceed; (3b) lost to racer -> exit 3' and update the proof line to 'All four cases covered'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add to the Scope that existing `test_binding_store.py` integer-comparison assertions on `recover_pending_bindings` (lines 162, 179, 186, 212, 214, 240) must be updated to unpack the new `(recovered, failures)` tuple, so the AC green-test gate is not broken.
findings.finding: The plan changes `recover_pending_bindings` return type from `int` to `tuple[int, int]`, but six existing integer-comparison tests in `test_binding_store.py` (lines 162, 179, 186, 212, 214, 240) compare the return value to integers (e.g. `== 0`, `== 1`). These tests will fail when the return type becomes a 2-tuple, yet the plan's Scope never explicitly calls out updating them.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section under `binding_store.py`, add: 'Update existing `test_binding_store.py` assertions that compare `recover_pending_bindings()` to a bare integer — change to `recovered, failures = store.recover_pending_bindings(client)` and assert on `recovered` accordingly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_binding_store.py` to the Scope edit-set and enumerate all `recover_pending_bindings` integer-return assertions (lines 162, 179, 186, 212, 240, 259) as requiring update to unpack the new `(recovered, failures)` tuple.
findings.finding: `recover_pending_bindings` currently returns `int`; the plan changes it to `tuple[int, int]`. Existing tests in `test_binding_store.py` (lines 162/164, 179/181, 186, 212/214, 240, 259) assert integer returns (`assert count == 1`, `== 0`). These tests are an unenumerated consumer of the signature change — they will fail after the plan is implemented.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: '`tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_binding_store.py`: update all `recover_pending_bindings` call-sites to unpack `(recovered, failures)` and assert each element individually.' Update each affected assertion e.g. `recovered, failures = store.recover_pending_bindings(client); assert recovered == 1`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC1 and the 'Why' section that the HTTPError retry extension in `_call_with_retry` covers REST-transport writes (add_label, set_entity_property, transitions — methods in acli_rest.py) but NOT `create_issue` (which routes through acli_subprocess._run_acli and raises CalledProcessError/AcliMutationError, not HTTPError); confirm the test in `test_retry_httperror.py` exercises a REST-path callable, not a mocked `create_issue`.
findings.finding: The `_call_with_retry` HTTPError retry extension will NOT benefit `create_issue` calls (the primary non-idempotent write), because `create_issue` routes through `acli_subprocess._run_acli` (a subprocess path) which raises `CalledProcessError`/`AcliMutationError`/`AcliTimeoutError` — never `urllib.error.HTTPError`. The plan's AC1 says this fixes 'the wrapped write path' and lists `dispatch_one.py:241` (which calls `_call_with_retry(client.create_issue, ...)`), implicitly claiming this protects creates from HTTPError 429 — but `client.create_issue` is an ACLI subprocess call, not a REST call. The actual benefit is to REST-path calls: `add_label`, `set_entity_property`, `transition_issue`, etc. The plan conflates the two transport layers in its problem statement ('The wrapped write path never retries transient HTTP failures').
findings.location: Why section item 4; AC1; dispatch_one.py:241; acli.py add_comment/create_issue routing
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, replace 'the wrapped write path' with 'REST-transport write operations (add_label, set_entity_property, transition_issue, set_parent — methods on AcliRestMixin)'; note that create_issue uses the subprocess transport and its 429 handling is already covered by acli_subprocess._rate_limit_backoff (ADR 0036). This removes the false implication without changing the implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the per-retry dedup guard bypasses or invalidates AcliClient._search_cache for the rebar-id JQL before each retry attempt (e.g., call `client._search_cache.pop(jql, None)` before the guard's search_issues call, or use a cache-bypassing REST search for the recovery path).
findings.finding: AcliClient.search_issues maintains an in-memory _search_cache keyed by JQL string (acli.py:391-428). The per-retry dedup guard (AC2) calls search_issues with the same rebar-id JQL as the initial pre-create check; on retry it will hit the cache and return the stale pre-create empty result, so the guard will never detect an issue that landed before the 429, defeating the duplicate-prevention guarantee the plan promises.
findings.suggested_fix: Before each retry's dedup search_issues call, evict the specific JQL key from client._search_cache (e.g., `getattr(client, '_search_cache', {}).pop(jql, None)`) so the retry issues a live Jira query. Alternatively, call client.get_issue_by_rest (the immediately-consistent REST GET path) using the known jira_key from the D1 keyed-pending record, which avoids JQL eventual-consistency entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test case to `test_lock_steal.py` (AC5) that sets `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_STEAL=OFF` (or equivalent falsy value) and asserts the held-lock path unconditionally exits 3 WITHOUT calling `steal_pass_lock`.
findings.finding: The `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_STEAL=OFF` behavior (unconditional exit-3 reversion, described as the kill-switch's back-out path in D3 and Scope) has no corresponding AC test case verifying that setting the env var OFF reverts to the pre-plan exit-3 behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_lock_steal.py`, add a parametrized case `env={REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_STEAL: '0'}` that monkeypatches `steal_pass_lock` to a sentinel, runs the held-lock path, and asserts (a) the sentinel was never called and (b) `SystemExit(3)` was raised.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in D1 / the dispatch_one.py scope whether a `bind_pending` persist failure is item-scoped (log + skip, continue the pass) or pass-scoped (halt), and add an explicit AC assertion for the chosen behavior.
findings.finding: When bind_pending persist fails, the plan says the create is aborted with a 'loud error', but does not specify whether the abort is item-scoped (skip this item, continue the pass) or pass-scoped (halt the entire reconciler run). The difference is material: a transient disk I/O error at pass-scope would kill an otherwise-healthy pass for all pending work items.
findings.location: Design decisions D1 / What section item 3 / Scope (dispatch_one.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Make it item-scoped: catch the persist exception in dispatch_one, log at ERROR, emit an alert_store bridge alert (same channel as recovery failures), record to mutation_failures, and continue to the next item — consistent with the fail-open philosophy stated in D4 for recovery_failures.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback schema state assertion to D1: trace what the old `recover_pending_bindings` does with EACH intermediate state (keyless-pending, keyed-pending-no-label, keyed-pending-with-label) after rollback, and confirm or fix the case where a keyed-pending record with no label yet applied is recoverable by the old code without orphaning the Jira issue.
findings.finding: The plan asserts rollback compatibility ('no corruption') but does not explicitly state the post-rollback schema STATE — specifically, what the binding store looks like and what the old `recover_pending_bindings` does with records that carry a non-null `jira_key` but where `add_label` has not yet been confirmed (the crash-between-key-persist-and-label-success case). T11 requires the plan assert on schema state AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0.
findings.suggested_fix: In D1, add a rollback state table: (1) keyless-pending → old code label-searches → finds nothing → unbinds (acceptable, no Jira issue created yet); (2) keyed-pending + no label → old code label-searches → finds nothing → **DATA LOSS RISK**: propose that the old code path fall back to direct key lookup (`get_issue(jira_key)`) when `jira_key` is non-null, or alternatively document that operators must manually apply the label before rolling back if any keyed-pending records exist. At minimum, add a MIGRATION NOTE warning operators to run `recover_pending_bindings` with the new code before rolling back.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete inter-module API by which `recovery_failures` crosses from `run_differs.py` to `reconcile.py` (e.g., 'add `recovery_failures: int` to `run_differs`'s return tuple' or 'pass a mutable result dict into `run_differs`'), so both files can be implemented consistently.
findings.finding: The `recovery_failures` counter's flow path from `run_differs.py` to `reconcile.py` is unspecified: D4 says 'run_differs captures `failures`, stores it on the pass context/return, and `reconcile.py` reads it into the `result` dict', but `run_differs.py` currently has no return value that `reconcile.py` reads for this kind of tally (verified: `run_differs.py:351` is a void call). The plan never names the mechanism (new return value from `run_differs`? a new field on a shared context object? a module-level side-channel?) by which `recovery_failures` crosses the `run_differs` → `reconcile.py` boundary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `bind_pending` persist failure is item-scoped (exception caught by `create_one`'s caller, `mutation_failures` +1, pass continues) or pass-scoped (propagates out of `run_differs`, entire pass fails), so the `mutation_failures` counter placement is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The `bind_pending` persist-failure abort scope is ambiguous: D1 says 'bind_pending persist failure is FATAL for that create (abort + loud error)' and AC3(c) says 'aborts + alerts', but neither the Scope section for `dispatch_one.py` nor D1 specifies whether the abort is item-scoped (raises an exception caught by `create_one`'s caller, item skipped, pass continues, `mutation_failures` incremented) or pass-scoped (propagates all the way out, entire pass fails). This placement ambiguity determines whether `mutation_failures` or some other counter gets the increment.
findings.location: Design decision D1; Scope: `dispatch_one.py`; AC3 sub-case (c)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the control-flow branch in D3/Scope: when `steal_pass_lock` returns a new oid (steal won), the implementation must set `lock_oid = stolen_oid` and `acquired = True` and jump directly to heartbeat setup, BYPASSING `acquire_pass_lock` — the plan must state this explicitly to prevent the implementer from calling acquire after a won steal.
findings.finding: After a successful steal(), the plan does not specify that acquire_pass_lock must be SKIPPED — the existing try: block unconditionally calls advisory.acquire_pass_lock(pass_id, repo_root), which will raise RefLockHeldError because the stolen lock is already held by pass_id. The plan's wiring description ('thread the new oid into lock_oid and start the heartbeat daemon against it, mirroring the acquire path') implies the implementer should jump directly to heartbeat setup, but no explicit control-flow change (e.g. a stolen_oid variable that short-circuits acquire_pass_lock) is specified.
findings.location: Design decisions D3; __main__.py:500-538 (check_pass_lock branch + try: block with acquire_pass_lock)
findings.suggested_fix: In D3, add: 'When steal_pass_lock returns a new oid, set acquired=True and lock_oid=stolen_oid, then jump to heartbeat setup at __main__.py:544 — do NOT call acquire_pass_lock (the lock is already held by this pass_id via the steal CAS). The check_pass_lock branch restructures as: if held and steal enabled -> attempt steal; if steal won -> set lock_oid, acquired=True, proceed to heartbeat; elif steal lost or disabled -> return 3.' Also add this to the Scope section's __main__.py bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether the 'None → re-read ref → acquire or exit-3' branching logic from D3 lives in `steal_pass_lock` (making it non-thin) or in `__main__.py`'s held-lock path (after `steal_pass_lock` returns None), so the wrapper's contract and placement are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The decision rule for handling `steal()`'s `None` return (re-read ref to discriminate 'free' vs. 'live holder') is specified in D3 but not co-located with the `steal_pass_lock` thin-wrapper spec in the Scope section for `_advisory_lock.py`. The Scope says only 'thin `steal_pass_lock(holder, repo_root, *, sleep_fn=time.sleep) -> str | None` wrapping `_ref_lock.steal()`', while D3 specifies the full branching logic (re-read → if free → acquire; if held → exit 3). Since `_ref_lock.steal()` returns `None` for BOTH 'ref now free' and 'live holder' cases (confirmed at `_ref_lock.py:531-533`), the discriminating re-read cannot be inside the thin wrapper without making it non-thin. The plan never explicitly states whether the re-read logic belongs in `steal_pass_lock` or in `__main__.py`'s held-lock path.
findings.location: Scope: `_advisory_lock.py` thin wrapper spec; Design decision D3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to the `_advisory_lock.py` Scope entry: 'The re-read-and-branch logic (None + freed → acquire; None + held → exit 3) lives in `__main__.py`'s held-lock path, NOT in `steal_pass_lock` itself; the wrapper is a pure pass-through with injected `sleep_fn`.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the complete updated write-ahead protocol as a numbered sequence in the plan (or explicitly in the `binding_store.py` Scope entry) so the `record_pending_key` + persist step appears unambiguously between `create_issue` and `add_label`.
findings.finding: The updated write-ahead protocol (with the new `record_pending_key` step between `create_issue` and `add_label`) is never spelled out as an ordered sequence in the plan. The Scope says 'update the module docstring to record the keyed-pending invariant', but the plan itself never gives the updated protocol enumeration. The existing docstring at `binding_store.py:8-16` has a 5-step sequence; the new protocol has at minimum 7 steps (bind_pending + persist, create_issue, record_pending_key + persist, add_label, bind_confirm, save). An implementor cannot verify they have the right sequence without reconstructing it from scattered D1 prose.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/binding_store.py entry: 'Updated protocol: 1. bind_pending(local_id) + save() [persist before create]; 2. create_issue(...) → jira_key; 3. record_pending_key(local_id, jira_key) + save() [persist before label]; 4. add_label(jira_key, rebar-id:local_id); 5. set_entity_property; 6. bind_confirm(local_id, jira_key); 7. save().'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the state transition a keyed-pending record undergoes after successful recovery in `recover_pending_bindings` (e.g., transition to `state='bound'` or remove the pending entry), and add a corresponding test assertion that the record is no longer `state='pending'` after a successful confirm.
findings.finding: The post-confirmation state transition of a successfully-recovered keyed-pending record is never specified: `record_pending_key` explicitly keeps `state='pending'`, and `recover_pending_bindings` 'confirms' the record, but the plan does not say what state the record moves to after successful recovery (e.g., transitioning to `state='bound'` or deleting the pending entry). Without this, every subsequent reconciler pass re-enters the recovery branch for the same already-resolved binding, producing repeated label-attach calls and spurious `alert_store` / recovery-failures accounting noise on every future pass.
findings.location: Design decision D1; binding_store.py scope; AC line 'crash after create+key-persist, before label -> next pass confirms, no duplicate'
findings.suggested_fix: After `recover_pending_bindings` successfully confirms a keyed-pending record (label attached + binding committed), call the existing binding confirmation path to transition the record to `state='bound'` (or equivalent terminal state) — the same path that a normal non-crashed create would follow. Document this in the `binding_store.py` module docstring alongside the keyed-pending invariant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a line to AC3 (or a note in the proof file `test_write_ahead_recovery.py`) explicitly verifying that `binding_store.py`'s module docstring documents the keyed-pending invariant (e.g., assert the docstring contains 'keyed-pending' or reference it in the PR checklist).
findings.finding: The `binding_store.py` module-docstring update (recording the keyed-pending invariant) is listed as a Scope deliverable but has no covering acceptance criterion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In D3 or the `__main__.py` Scope bullet, explicitly acknowledge whether `pass_id` is currently used or logged between its generation site and the lock acquisition, and confirm the hoist does not change observable structured-log or downstream semantics (or note it as an intentional change).
findings.finding: The plan hoists `pass_id` generation above the `check_pass_lock` block (to make it available as the steal `holder`) but does not acknowledge whether `pass_id` is currently emitted in structured logs or used by any downstream code under an assumption that it only exists after lock acquisition — making the reordering a potentially unacknowledged observable behavior change.
findings.location: Scope (__main__.py bullet: 'hoist pass_id generation ABOVE the check_pass_lock block'); D3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_STEAL` (default ON, reverts to exit-3) to the project's env-var config reference, README, or the relevant runbook section so operators can discover and use the kill-switch without reading source.
findings.finding: The new `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_STEAL` env var (kill-switch, default ON) is not listed in any config reference, runbook, or operator-facing doc update within the plan's Scope — operators and on-call engineers have no documented way to discover or use this escape hatch without reading the source.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line entry to the existing env-var config table / `docs/runbooks/reconciler.md` (or equivalent): `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_STEAL` — `1` (default): attempt `steal()` on held lock; `0`: unconditional exit 3 (pre-plan behaviour).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either confirm ADR 0036 already covers `urllib.error.HTTPError` retry scope (and cite the specific section in the plan), or add an explicit 'update ADR 0036 to extend retry policy to HTTPError 429/5xx' task to the Scope section.
findings.finding: The plan cites 'ADR 0036' as governing the retry-backoff formula but does not specify whether ADR 0036 currently covers the new `urllib.error.HTTPError` retry scope or needs to be updated — if ADR 0036 only documents the existing `JiraAPIError` path, the ADR becomes stale and the extension is undocumented at the architectural level.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a clearly-labelled section in an existing reconciler design doc / CLAUDE.md) capturing the D3 lease-steal decision: rationale for steal() over exit-3, the three outcome branches, the kill-switch, and the heartbeat-wiring contract.
findings.finding: The lease-steal mechanism (D3) — which fundamentally changes the held-lock path from unconditional exit-3 to a three-branch steal/acquire/exit flow — is a novel architectural decision captured only in the plan's Design Decisions section, with no ADR or CLAUDE.md update specified in the Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one env-var kill-switch (e.g., `REBAR_RECONCILER_RETRY_HTTPERROR`, defaulting ON) for the HTTPError retry extension (Fix 1), mirroring the REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_STEAL pattern for Fix 4, so operators can fast-revert retry behavior without a redeploy.
findings.finding: Fixes 1 (HTTPError retry extension), 2 (create dedup guard), and 3 (write-ahead bind_pending) are deployed as a single 100%-traffic flip with no feature flag, canary, or staged rollout mechanism. Only Fix 4 (lease steal) has a kill-switch (REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_STEAL). If any of the three unflagged fixes causes unexpected behavior in production (e.g., a previously-propagated raw HTTPError is now silently retried on a path where callers depended on fast-fail, or the JQL dedup search misfires), there is no operator lever to disable it without a full redeploy.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `REBAR_RECONCILER_RETRY_HTTPERROR` (default ON) checked inside `_call_with_retry` before the HTTPError retry branch. For Fix 3, a `REBAR_RECONCILER_WRITE_AHEAD` flag (default ON) that bypasses bind_pending/record_pending_key and falls back to the old in-memory-only path covers the write-ahead rollback case. These flags should be tested in the existing test files alongside the positive-path tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the identical `Retry-After`-honor + `2**(attempt+1)+rand[0,1)` + 60s-cap formula in `_call_with_retry` should be extracted to a shared helper (e.g. `_advisory_backoff_delay(attempt, retry_after_header) -> float`) rather than duplicating `acli_subprocess._rate_limit_backoff`'s logic — or document explicitly why the two copies are intentionally kept separate (e.g. different detection mechanism warrants different code path).
findings.finding: The backoff formula for the new HTTPError retry branch in `_call_with_retry` (Retry-After-honor + `2**(attempt+1)+rand[0,1)` fallback + 60s cap) is byte-for-byte identical to the already-implemented `_rate_limit_backoff()` in `acli_subprocess.py`. The plan re-specifies this formula inline in `_call_with_retry` rather than extracting it to a shared helper — introducing two diverging copies of the same ADR-0036 backoff logic.
findings.location: What / item 1 (backoff formula spec); AC item 1; `acli_subprocess.py:122-144` (`_rate_limit_backoff`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `apply_inbound.py` and `apply_inbound_records.py` as blast-radius consumers of the `_call_with_retry` HTTPError-retry change in the Scope section, and explicitly confirm (or bound) the new retry behavior for their `_call_with_retry` call-sites.
findings.finding: `apply_inbound.py` and `apply_inbound_records.py` both import `_call_with_retry` from `batch_dispatch` (which re-exports from `dispatch_one`). The plan's extension of `_call_with_retry` to retry raw `urllib.error.HTTPError` 429/5xx will silently change the retry behavior of all `_call_with_retry` call-sites in these two files — but neither file is enumerated in the plan's Scope or acknowledged as a blast-radius consumer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `binding_store.py` docstring update to ADR 0027 (`docs/adr/0027-reconciler-binding-lifecycle.md`) documenting the new keyed-pending sub-state (`state='pending'` + non-null `jira_key`), the extended write-ahead protocol (`bind_pending → create → record_pending_key+persist → add_label → bind_confirm`), and the deterministic recovery branch — so the authoritative state-machine reference stays consistent with the implementation.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new binding sub-state — `state='pending'` with non-null `jira_key` ('keyed-pending') — that extends the ADR 0027 state machine (`pending | confirmed | retired`), but only documents this in the `binding_store.py` module docstring. ADR 0027 remains the authoritative reference for the binding lifecycle and will be stale after this change, which is a novel architectural extension of a named prior-art design.
findings.location: Design decision D1; Scope section (`binding_store.py` docstring); ADR 0027 (`docs/adr/0027-reconciler-binding-lifecycle.md`)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add `docs/adr/0027-reconciler-binding-lifecycle.md` to the list of files changed (read-only → write): append a new section or amend the existing protocol description to document the four-state write-ahead sequence and the `keyed-pending` recovery path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether the four audit sub-fixes (retry layer, create-dedup/write-ahead, recovery wiring, lease steal) can be sequenced as stacked-but-independent Gerrit changes rather than a single bundle, given each is independently valuable, testable, and releasable — or document a tighter coherence argument beyond rebase-avoidance (e.g. a runtime dependency that makes them worthless apart).
findings.finding: The plan bundles four independently-valuable, independently-testable, and independently-releasable safety fixes (retry layer, create dedup guard + write-ahead, recovery wiring, lease steal), each with its own audit sub-finding number, its own named test file, and — for fix 4 — its own kill-switch. The plan's bundling justification is developer convenience ('avoids four near-trivial stacked changes with repeated rebase/review overhead'), which is a process argument, not a coherence argument. The plan itself admits each AC is 'independently verifiable, so a reviewer can accept/reject any fix on its own test,' which directly concedes the independence criterion for decomposition.
findings.location: Sequencing section; Acceptance Criteria (four independent test proof files)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
